title: Permanent Neurofeedback for ONUVA Children's Home
population: Orphaned children, trauma-affected youth, and school staff
whatWeFund: One permanent CalmWaves direct neurofeedback machine — giving ONUVA full ownership of the technology they have been using, so sessions can continue without interruption for every child and staff member in the home.
summary: ONUVA Children's Home in El Salvador has been running daily neurofeedback sessions for over two years — with remarkable results. 97% of participants showed measurable improvement. 18,000+ sessions delivered. The machine they've been using belongs to CalmWaves, which has generously allowed the school to continue while they seek permanent funding. This project changes that. $10,000 purchases the machine outright and secures the program permanently.
whyThisPopulation:
The children at ONUVA are orphans or come from backgrounds of violence, poverty, and family separation. Many arrived with nervous systems locked in survival mode — unable to sleep, learn, or feel safe. The teachers and house parents who care for them carry their own unresolved trauma. What makes the ONUVA model powerful is that everyone receives sessions — children and adults alike. When the adults heal, the environment changes. When the environment changes, the children have a real chance.

Direct neurofeedback reaches what talk therapy cannot. It works at the neurological level, helping the brain regulate itself without requiring the child to verbalize or relive trauma. For kids who have never had the words — or the safety — to speak about what happened to them, this is what healing actually looks like.

title: Direct Neurofeedback in the Philippines
whatWeFund: 3 CalmWaves calm boxes, 5-day practitioner training, 12 months of remote support and outcome tracking
summary: Manila is home to one of Southeast Asia's largest populations of trafficking survivors — many of whom receive immediate rescue support but never access long-term trauma care. We have identified a partner facility with the capacity, staff, and commitment to run a full neurofeedback program. This deployment would be the first in Southeast Asia.
whyThisPopulation: Trafficking survivors carry complex trauma in their nervous systems long after rescue. Talk therapy rarely reaches the root. Direct neurofeedback works at the neurological level — calming the fight-or-flight response that keeps survivors stuck. These are people who need it most and have the least access to care.

title: 6 Calm Boxes at Casa Grande Youth Facility
whatWeFund: 6 CalmWaves calm boxes, full staff training for 4 practitioners, 12 months of ongoing support and outcome tracking
summary: Casa Grande houses youth ages 12–17 who have been failed by nearly every system designed to help them. Most carry untreated trauma from abuse, neglect, and violence. The facility has committed leadership and staff who are ready to run a full program — they just need the technology.
whyThisPopulation: Incarcerated youth are among the most trauma-affected and least-served populations in the country. Traditional therapy is scarce inside facilities. Direct neurofeedback can be administered on-site, requires no verbal disclosure, and produces measurable results within the first few sessions.
